name: fertility_clinic_embryo_blackout
premise: Medical director Dr. Hannah Ko is overseeing a fertility clinic after a rolling blackout damaged one of the cryogenic storage systems overnight. The emergency team managed to stabilize most tanks, but twelve embryos from different families experienced a temperature excursion that may have compromised viability. One couple has already exhausted every funded IVF cycle and is due to transfer this week; another family has a court order in a divorce case requiring the embryos remain preserved until ownership is resolved. The clinic's insurer and attorneys want Hannah to disclose only the affected embryos that testing confirms are nonviable, arguing that premature alarm will trigger lawsuits and destroy trust in a clinic that still preserved thousands of embryos successfully. The embryology staff insists every impacted family deserves immediate disclosure, even if the science is uncertain and some embryos may still survive. Hannah must decide whether to notify all affected families now or wait for confirmatory viability testing that could take three days.

agents.name: Dr. Hannah Ko
agents.identity: I am the clinic's medical director and have spent my career caring for patients whose hopes are bound up with microscopic lives and fragile timing. I know that transparency can be devastating, but silence can become its own form of injury when people are denied the chance to act.
agents.goal: I want to treat every family fairly while minimizing avoidable panic and preserving the chance to make medically sound decisions. I need to decide whether uncertain truth should be shared immediately or only after stronger evidence reduces the chance of misleading people.

agents.name: Mira Salcedo
agents.identity: I am the senior embryologist who watched the alarms fail and then stood beside those tanks while we fought to stabilize them. The families entrusted us with possibilities they may never be able to create again, and uncertainty does not erase that responsibility.
agents.goal: I want Hannah to notify every affected family immediately because mercy means letting them prepare, grieve, or intervene as soon as risk is known rather than protecting the clinic from distress. I believe withholding uncertain bad news still treats patients as outsiders to their own futures.

choices: I will notify all affected families immediately that their embryos may have been compromised, even though viability is still uncertain and panic will follow.
I will wait for confirmatory testing before notifying families so I do not burden them with potentially misleading information while the evidence is incomplete.
forced_choice.call_to_action: Dr. Hannah Ko must decide whether to notify all affected families now or wait for embryo viability testing. What does Dr. Hannah Ko do?
